planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in deep shade to partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in zone 10–12; Spicate Calyptrocalyx is a striking, clustering, understory palm native to the lowland and hill rainforests of New Guinea, grown for its highly attractive, bright orange to red new leaf growth (new fronds emerge copper, orange, and red before maturing to green) and its excellent tolerance of deep shade — outstanding for shaded tropical garden positions.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in deep to moderate shade; Calyptrocalyx spicatus requires consistently high humidity and moisture — mulch heavily, water regularly, and maintain shade protection; excellent for tropical understory and shade garden settings. Calyptrocalyx spicatus (Spicate Calyptrocalyx or New Guinea Understory Palm) is a clustering, suckering, small to medium understory palm native to the humid lowland and hill rainforests of New Guinea (Papua New Guinea and Irian Jaya), grown primarily for an ornamental characteristic shared by many Calyptrocalyx species: the spectacular orange-to-red coloration of emerging new fronds. Each new leaf unfurls in bright copper, orange, or red tones before gradually maturing to standard deep green — creating a continuously colorful display as new leaves emerge throughout the growing season. This 'colored new growth' trait makes Calyptrocalyx spicatus an exceptionally attractive shade-garden palm. The multiple clustering stems are slender, graceful, and reach moderate heights (4–10 feet). The species is also notable for its tolerance of deep shade — genuinely adapted to the rainforest floor — making it useful for shaded positions where most palms would struggle. Calyptrocalyx spicatus is rarely seen in North American collections due to limited distribution, but is cultivated by tropical palm enthusiasts in South Florida and Hawaii for its remarkable new-growth coloration.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Calyptrocalyx spicatus
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — New Guinea Palms Collection

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in zone 10–12; Solitaire Palm is an elegant, slender, solitary-trunked palm native to the tropical and subtropical rainforests of Queensland, Australia — producing a compact, formal crown of arching, gracefully pinnate fronds above a clean, prominently ring-marked trunk, and bearing small, bright-red ornamental fruits in drooping clusters beneath the crown shaft.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; Ptychosperma elegans grows well in partial shade when young — excellent for planting under light tree canopy — and can be moved to more sun as it establishes; water regularly and fertilize monthly with palm-specific fertilizer. Ptychosperma elegans (Solitaire Palm or Seaforthia Palm) is a slender, elegant, single-trunked feather palm native to the tropical and subtropical rainforests and forest margins of coastal Queensland, Australia — from Cairns south through the Wet Tropics to Brisbane — where it typically grows in moist, well-drained positions in lowland and lower-montane rainforest. The palm is distinguished by its exceptional formal elegance: a smooth, prominently ring-marked, pencil-slender trunk supporting a compact, tidy crown of arching, pinnate, bright-green fronds with a characteristic 'jagged' appearance to the leaflet tips (fish-tailed, or praemorse, leaflet ends) that give the fronds a slightly irregular but attractive quality. Masses of small, brilliant-red fruits in drooping infructescences appear periodically and add ornamental interest. Ptychosperma elegans is tolerant of partial shade, making it an excellent choice for shaded or filtered-light positions in zone 10–12 gardens, and it also adapts to full sun in humid coastal conditions. Widely planted in Hawaii, South Florida, Caribbean gardens, and tropical Asia as a refined, formal landscape accent.
sources: Australian Native Plants Society — Ptychosperma elegans
University of Florida IFAS — Ptychosperma elegans

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in well-drained, deep, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in zone 8–11; Silver Date Palm is the most widely distributed wild date palm of the Indian subcontinent — native to India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka — producing edible, small, orange-to-dark-brown dates and a fermented sap (toddy) of cultural and economic importance, while also being one of the most cold-hardy and drought-tolerant ornamental date palms available.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, deep, alkaline-tolerant soil; Phoenix sylvestris is drought tolerant once established and adapts well to alkaline, calcareous, and slightly saline soils — water regularly only in establishment; excellent for hot, dry, alkaline landscape positions in zone 8–11. Phoenix sylvestris (Silver Date Palm, Indian Date Palm, or Wild Date Palm) is a large, majestic, single-trunked feather palm native to the plains and hills of the Indian subcontinent — India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Nepal, and Sri Lanka — where it is one of the most culturally and economically important wild palms. The silvery-gray crown of long, recurving, pinnate fronds gives the palm a distinctively glaucous, blue-silver appearance that differs from the common date palm (P. dactylifera). The fruits are small (1 inch), orange-to-brown dates — edible but less sweet and more fibrous than commercial dates. The primary economic use is 'toddy' production: tapping the crown to collect sweet sap, which is fermented into palm wine, distilled into arrack, or boiled into jaggery (palm sugar). Phoenix sylvestris is substantially more cold-hardy than many date palms — tolerating temperatures to 18–22°F when established — making it excellent for zone 8b–9 date-palm landscapes. Outstanding ornamental and shade palm for Gulf Coast, Southwest, and Mediterranean-climate gardens.
sources: Indian Council of Forestry Research — Phoenix sylvestris
FAO Non-Wood Forest Products — Phoenix sylvestris (Toddy Palm)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, deep, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in zone 8–11; Cliff Date Palm is a graceful, medium-sized date palm native to rocky cliff faces and hill forests of the eastern Himalayas (Sikkim, Bhutan, Bangladesh, Assam) — producing remarkably elegant, arching, dark-green, soft-textured fronds and edible, small, orange date-like fruits, with considerably more cold-hardiness and shade tolerance than the common date palm.

directions: Plant in well-drained, deep, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Phoenix rupicola is among the more shade-tolerant date palms — adapting to filtered light beneath taller trees — and tolerates moderate drought when established; water deeply but infrequently in subtropical and Mediterranean conditions. Phoenix rupicola (Cliff Date Palm or Rupicola Date Palm) is one of the most elegantly formed and ornamentally refined of all Phoenix date palm species, native to the rocky escarpments, cliff faces, and hill forests of the eastern Himalayan foothills — Sikkim, Bhutan, Assam, and Meghalaya in northeast India, and Bangladesh. The common name 'Cliff Date Palm' accurately describes its native habitat: the palm naturally grows in rock crevices and on steep, exposed cliff faces at elevations of 1,500–5,000 feet. The fronds are the defining ornamental feature: extraordinarily graceful, arching, and soft-textured — lacking the stiff, rigid quality of many Phoenix palms — with a deep, rich green color and a naturally weeping, 'fountain' form that makes the plant immediately distinctive in a landscape setting. Phoenix rupicola shows more shade tolerance than most date palms due to its cliff-forest habitat and is one of the better date palms for partially shaded landscape positions. The small, orange, date-like fruits are eaten locally. Among the best date palms for compact landscapes, courtyard gardens, and partial-shade positions in zones 8–11.
sources: Royal Botanic Garden Kew — Phoenix rupicola

subcategory: Egyptian and East African branching palm

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in very well-drained, sandy to rocky, alkaline to neutral, arid soil in zone 8–11; Doum Palm is one of the only true branching palms in the world — native to Egypt, the Nile Valley, and sub-Saharan Africa — with naturally dichotomously branching trunks producing multiple growing tips, and bearing large, orange-brown, gingerbread-flavored fruits sacred to ancient Egypt and still an important food across northeastern Africa.

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained, sandy to gravelly, alkaline, lean, arid soil; Hyphaene thebaica is adapted to extreme desert and semi-arid conditions — drought tolerant, heat tolerant, alkaline-soil adapted, and saline-tolerant; avoid clay or wet soils. Hyphaene thebaica (Doum Palm, Gingerbread Palm, or Egyptian Doum) is a large, majestic, fan palm native to the Nile Valley, northeastern Africa, and the dry savannas and deserts of sub-Saharan Africa from Egypt and Sudan through East Africa to South Africa — one of the most distinctive and botanically remarkable palms in the world because of its naturally dichotomously branching trunk. Unlike virtually all other true palms (which are monocots and cannot branch), Hyphaene thebaica produces true dichotomous bifurcation at each growing point, creating a naturally multi-headed, tree-like branching architecture that makes it instantly recognizable and utterly unlike any other palm. The large (2–4 inch), smooth, orange-brown fruits have a hard, starchy, dry, reddish inner mesocarp with a flavor and texture remarkably similar to gingerbread — giving the common name 'Gingerbread Palm.' The fruits were a significant food source in ancient Egypt — doum fruit has been found in Egyptian tombs dating to 3200 BCE and is depicted in reliefs at Luxor and Karnak. The hard endosperm ('vegetable ivory') is carved into buttons and small ornaments. Excellent accent palm for hot, dry, alkaline-soil landscapes in zone 8–11.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Hyphaene thebaica
FAO Non-Wood Forest Products — Doum Palm

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in very well-drained, deep, sandy to loamy, well-drained, alkaline to neutral soil in zone 9–12; African Fan Palm is the African counterpart of the Asian Toddy Palm (Borassus flabellifer) — a massive, stately, long-lived fan palm native to the savanna, woodland, and floodplain regions of sub-Saharan Africa from Senegal to Kenya and south to Zimbabwe, producing very large, heavy, orange-brown fruits whose jelly-like germination tissue, fleshy mesocarp, and young shoots are all consumed as food.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, deep, sandy to loamy, alkaline-tolerant soil; Borassus aethiopum is drought tolerant and adapted to seasonal dry periods of African savanna — water regularly only in the first few years of establishment; growth is very slow initially as the palm develops its extensive root system before vertical growth. Borassus aethiopum (African Fan Palm or African Palmyra) is one of the largest and most ecologically important palms of sub-Saharan Africa — a massive, solitary-trunked fan palm native to the savanna, woodland, and seasonal floodplain ecosystems of tropical and subtropical Africa from Senegal east through Nigeria, Sudan, Kenya, Tanzania, and south to Zimbabwe and Mozambique. The closely related Asian Toddy Palm (Borassus flabellifer) is a separate species adapted to South and Southeast Asian monsoon climates. The African Fan Palm reaches impressive dimensions — trunks to 80+ feet topped by a large, rounded crown of enormous, pleated, circular fan fronds up to 10 feet across. The large (4–6 inch), smooth, orange-brown fruits are economically important: the jelly-like germinating seed tissue is eaten by children and livestock; the fleshy mesocarp is eaten raw or cooked; palm sap tapped from the crown is fermented into 'African palm wine.' The hard, extremely dense, durable black palm heartwood is used for construction, tool handles, and musical instruments across its range. A dramatic, long-lived specimen palm for large tropical landscapes in zone 9–12 with patience — first 5–10 years of growth are slow as the root system establishes.
sources: FAO Non-Wood Forest Products — African Borassus Palm
